  • Hide All Code

Visions of Technological Futures in African National AI Strategies versus EU Governance Frameworks

Peace Onebunne

February 2026

LIBRARIES

I load all libraries in a single chunk at the top of the document so that any reader can immediately see what the analysis depends on, and so that missing packages surface as a single, early error rather than cryptically mid-analysis.

tidyverse - the backbone of all data manipulation and ggplot2 visualisation tidytext - provides tidy-friendly tokenisation, TF-IDF, and stop-word removal text2vec - efficient sparse-matrix TF-IDF and cosine similarity at scale lsa - latent semantic analysis and additional cosine utilities pdftools - PDF text extraction, replacing pdfminer from the Python pipeline stringr - consistent, readable regex operations forcats - factor reordering in visualisations ggplot2 - declarative visualisation (bundled in tidyverse but called out explicitly) scales - axis formatting helpers knitr - for kable tables in the rendered output kableExtra - enhanced styling for kable tables broom - tidy model output for t-tests and regressions rstatix - pipe-friendly statistical tests (Wilcoxon, effect sizes) ggpubr - publication-ready ggplot2 extensions including significance brackets pheatmap - clustered heatmaps for the similarity matrices viridis - perceptually uniform colour palettes (accessible)

library(tidyverse)
library(tidytext)
library(text2vec)
library(lsa)
library(pdftools)
library(stringr)
library(forcats)
library(scales)
library(knitr)
library(kableExtra)
library(broom)
library(rstatix)
library(ggpubr)
library(pheatmap)
library(viridis)
library(here)
library(quanteda)
library(ggplot2)
library(quanteda.textplots)
library(tidyr)
library(dplyr)
library(textstem)


peace.theme <- ggplot2::theme_bw() +
  ggplot2::theme(
    axis.line = ggplot2::element_line(colour = "black"),
    panel.grid.minor = ggplot2::element_blank(),
    panel.grid.major = ggplot2::element_blank(),
    panel.border = ggplot2::element_blank(),
    panel.background = ggplot2::element_blank(),
    legend.title = ggplot2::element_blank()
  )

print.me <- function(x, ...) {
  len <- min(nrow(x), 200)
  x[1:len, , drop = FALSE] |>
    kbl(digits = 2, align = "l", booktabs = TRUE) |>
    kable_styling(fixed_thead = TRUE) |>
    kable_paper("striped", 
                full_width = TRUE, 
                html_font = "Helvetica", 
                font_size = 12) |>
    row_spec(
      0, 
      color = "white", 
      background = "#9D2235", 
      font_size = 12) |>
    scroll_box(
      width = "700px", 
      height = "500px") |>
    asis_output()
}

registerS3method("knit_print", "data.frame", print.me)

DESIGN PALETTE — DARK BLUE GRADIENT

I define the colour palette here, centrally, so that every plot in the document draws from the same system. Changing one hex value here cascades through the entire document.

The gradient moves from near-black navy (#0A0F2C) through royal blue (#1A3A8F) to a lighter steel blue (#4A90C4) and an accent sky (#7EC8E3). This is intentional: dark tones carry weight in regulatory discourse visualisations, while the lighter end marks contrast groups (EU vs Africa).

pal <- list(
  dark1   = "#0A0F2C",   # near-black navy — strongest emphasis
  dark2   = "#0D1B4B",   # deep navy
  mid1    = "#1A3A8F",   # royal blue — primary bars/lines
  mid2    = "#2B5BB5",   # medium blue
  light1  = "#4A90C4",   # steel blue — secondary elements
  light2  = "#7EC8E3",   # sky blue — contrast group or annotations
  accent  = "#A8D8EA",   # pale blue — backgrounds, fills
  neutral = "#E8EEF7"    # near-white blue — panel backgrounds
)

# I also define a two-group vector for region comparisons.
# Africa is assigned the deeper blue; EU the lighter.
region_colours <- c(
  "Africa" = pal$mid1,
  "EU"     = pal$light1
)

# A gradient function for heatmaps that runs dark → light within the navy family.
heatmap_palette <- colorRampPalette(c(pal$dark1, pal$mid2, pal$light1, pal$accent))(100)

CLEANING FUNCTION: peace_genie_pro

This function is the first thing applied to every raw PDF before any analysis. It standardises encoding, normalises quotation marks, removes URLs and emails, lowercases, strips numbers and punctuation, and collapses whitespace. The result is a clean, reproducible text object ready for corpus ingestion and lexicon scoring.

Each parameter is a logical toggle so the function can be called at different pipeline stages — for example, keeping hyphens intact during an initial inspection pass and only splitting them in the final run.

Arguments: text_input character vector (single string or multi-line) to_lower convert to lowercase (default TRUE) fix_encoding re-encode to UTF-8, substituting unreadable bytes normalize_quotes replace curly/smart apostrophes with ASCII ’ remove_urls strip http/https and www patterns remove_emails strip email address patterns remove_numbers strip digit sequences split_hyphens replace hyphens and underscores with spaces squash_whitespace collapse whitespace runs to a single space

Returns: a single cleaned character string.

peace_genie_pro <- function(text_input,
                            to_lower          = TRUE,
                            fix_encoding      = TRUE,
                            normalize_quotes  = TRUE,
                            remove_urls       = TRUE,
                            remove_emails     = TRUE,
                            remove_numbers    = TRUE,
                            split_hyphens     = TRUE,
                            squash_whitespace = TRUE) {

  # Collapse multi-line input to a single string.
  # This makes the function safe whether it receives readLines() output
  # or a pre-collapsed character vector.
  if (length(text_input) > 1) {
    text_input <- paste(text_input, collapse = " ")
  }
  text_input <- as.character(text_input)

  # Re-encode to UTF-8. PDFs — especially scanned policy documents from
  # African national ministries — frequently carry encoding artefacts that
  # break downstream regex operations silently.
  if (fix_encoding) {
    text_input <- iconv(text_input, from = "", to = "UTF-8", sub = " ")
  }

  # Normalise curly apostrophes to plain ASCII '.
  # Policy PDFs consistently use smart quotes; leaving them uncorrected
  # fragments tokens like "don\u2019t" into two pieces.
  if (normalize_quotes) {
    text_input <- gsub(
      "[\u2018\u2019\u02BC\u201B\uFF07]", "'", text_input
    )
  }

  # Lowercase before URL/email removal.
  # Having consistent case prevents double-counting of tokens like
  # "Data" and "data" in frequency-based analyses.
  if (to_lower) {
    text_input <- tolower(text_input)
  }

  # Remove URLs. The pattern captures both protocol-prefixed and bare
  # www addresses. Policy documents often include hyperlinks in footnotes
  # that would otherwise appear as high-frequency tokens.
  if (remove_urls) {
    text_input <- gsub("https?://\\S+|www\\.\\S+", " ", text_input)
  }

  # Remove email addresses. Author contact details in appendices would
  # otherwise survive as spurious tokens.
  if (remove_emails) {
    text_input <- gsub(
      "\\b[[:alnum:]._%+-]+@[[:alnum:].-]+\\.[[:alpha:]]{2,}\\b",
      " ", text_input
    )
  }

  # Replace hyphens and underscores with spaces.
  # PDFs introduce hyphenation artefacts at line breaks (e.g. "govern-ance").
  # Splitting ensures the tokeniser handles each part separately.
  if (split_hyphens) {
    text_input <- gsub("[-_]", " ", text_input)
  }

  # Remove digit sequences. Article numbers, dates, and page references
  # in policy documents carry no semantic meaning for imaginary lexicon
  # analysis and inflate document length, distorting normalisation.
  if (remove_numbers) {
    text_input <- gsub("[0-9]+", " ", text_input)
  }

  # Remove all remaining punctuation. Applied after number removal so
  # decimal points are already gone before the period pattern fires.
  text_input <- gsub("[[:punct:]]+", " ", text_input)

  # Collapse whitespace. Every substitution above leaves single spaces
  # in its wake; this step merges them and strips leading/trailing
  # whitespace so the returned string is immediately ready for corpus ingestion.
  if (squash_whitespace) {
    text_input <- gsub("\\s+", " ", text_input)
    text_input <- trimws(text_input)
  }

  return(text_input)
}

CORPUS INGESTION

The corpus was assembled and cleaned in the Python pipeline (Future.ipynb) using pdftools-equivalent PDF extraction and peace_genie_pro-equivalent cleaning. The output was serialised to CSV at Docs/Analysis_Output/corpus_clean_future.csv. I load directly from that CSV here rather than re-extracting from PDFs, because the Python pipeline handles the OCR and encoding issues in the scanned African ministry PDFs more reliably than R’s pdftools on this corpus. The peace_genie_pro function defined above is retained in this document as a methodological record of the cleaning decisions applied in that pipeline.

df <- read_csv(
  "C:/Users/amaka/OneDrive - Temple University/PhD/Year 1b/AI Governance/Docs/Analysis_Output/corpus_clean_future.csv",
  show_col_types = FALSE
) %>%
  mutate(
    region     = factor(region,   levels = c("Africa", "EU")),
    doc_type   = factor(doc_type, levels = c("act", "framework")),
    clean_text = replace_na(as.character(clean_text), "")
  ) %>%
  filter(str_trim(clean_text) != "")

cat("Corpus loaded:", nrow(df), "documents\n")
## Corpus loaded: 31 documents
print(table(df$region, df$doc_type))
##         
##          act framework
##   Africa  20         9
##   EU       2         0

CORPUS INTEGRITY CHECK

I re-enforce factor levels and drop any document with empty clean_text before analysis begins. The corpus contains 29 African documents (20 acts, 9 frameworks) and 2 EU documents (both acts). The EU corpus is intentionally smaller: the EU AI Act and GDPR are the primary instruments through which the Brussels Effect operates in this domain. The corpus asymmetry is a structural feature of the research design and is discussed as a limitation in the paper.

df <- df %>%
  mutate(
    region     = factor(region,   levels = c("Africa", "EU")),
    doc_type   = factor(doc_type, levels = c("act", "framework")),
    clean_text = replace_na(as.character(clean_text), "")
  ) %>%
  filter(str_trim(clean_text) != "")

cat("Corpus ready.\n")
## Corpus ready.
cat("Total documents:", nrow(df), "\n")
## Total documents: 31
print(table(df$region, df$doc_type))
##         
##          act framework
##   Africa  20         9
##   EU       2         0

LEXICON CONSTRUCTION — THEORY-DRIVEN, TEXT-DERIVED

Both hypotheses require a vocabulary that is derived from the theoretical argument rather than imported from a generic sentiment or policy dictionary. H1 concerns discursive convergence — the reproduction of EU governance vocabulary in African AI strategies, consistent with a Brussels Effect operating at the level of regulatory language. H2 concerns infrastructural imaginaries — the foregrounding of material conditions (compute, connectivity, cloud dependency, sovereignty over technical substrate) that are absent or assumed away in EU instruments.

I therefore build six lexicon categories directly from the constructs in the hypotheses:

  1. IMAGINARY TERMS capture future-oriented, aspirational language that encodes a vision of AI-driven technological modernity. These are the terms that circulate a shared governance imaginary — innovation, frontier, transformation, vision. H1 predicts these will show high lexical convergence between African and EU documents because the Brussels Effect exports precisely this kind of aspirational framing.

  2. INFRASTRUCTURAL TERMS capture the material grounding language specific to H2 — electricity, connectivity, data centers, cloud, hardware, skills. H2 predicts African strategies score significantly higher on these because they are articulating governance under conditions of material constraint that EU instruments take for granted.

  3. DATA TERMS capture the regulatory architecture of data governance: personal data, consent, controller, data subject, GDPR-derived vocabulary. H1 predicts high convergence here because the Brussels Effect is most documented in the data protection domain.

  4. RIGHTS AND ETHICS TERMS capture the normative vocabulary the EU exports through the AI Act and related instruments: fundamental rights, dignity, fairness, accountability, transparency. Again H1 predicts convergence.

  5. DEVELOPMENT TERMS capture the developmental state imaginary distinctive to African strategic documents: leapfrog, capacity building, inclusion, agriculture, health, national. H2 predicts higher density in Africa.

  6. SOVEREIGNTY TERMS capture the technical substrate dimension of H2: digital sovereignty, strategic autonomy, data sovereignty, independence. H2 predicts higher density in African strategies reflecting the asymmetric power relations under which African governance is articulated.

All scores are normalised by document word count so that longer documents do not inflate scores. Word boundaries (\b) are enforced in all regex patterns to prevent partial matches.

LEXICONS <- list(

  imaginary = c(
    "innovation", "future", "transformation", "leadership",
    "global", "competitiveness", "frontier", "growth",
    "vision", "opportunity", "pioneering", "revolution",
    "advancement", "progress", "aspiration", "ambitious",
    "strategy", "potential", "horizon", "emerging"
  ),

  infrastructural = c(
    "infrastructure", "capacity", "electricity", "data center",
    "connectivity", "internet", "skills", "training",
    "bandwidth", "network", "server", "cloud", "hardware",
    "power", "deployment", "access", "resources", "gap",
    "readiness", "facility"
  ),

  data = c(
    "data", "personal data", "data protection", "privacy",
    "consent", "processing", "controller", "processor",
    "data subject", "anonymisation", "pseudonymisation",
    "data flow", "transfer", "storage", "collection",
    "retention", "breach", "security", "encryption"
  ),

  rights_ethics = c(
    "rights", "human rights", "fundamental rights", "dignity",
    "fairness", "bias", "discrimination", "accountability",
    "transparency", "ethics", "ethical", "principle",
    "well being", "harm", "safety", "protection",
    "inclusion", "access", "equity", "justice"
  ),

  development = c(
    "development", "economic growth", "leapfrog", "inclusion",
    "poverty", "sustainable", "welfare", "employment",
    "agriculture", "health", "education", "public service",
    "national", "continent", "africa", "african",
    "capacity building", "investment", "sector", "industry"
  ),

  sovereignty = c(
    "sovereignty", "independence", "autonomy",
    "national", "local control", "self reliance",
    "strategic autonomy", "digital sovereignty",
    "ownership", "governance", "self determination",
    "indigenous", "local", "domestic"
  )
)

# I print the lexicon sizes so a reader can see the scope of each category.
cat("Lexicon sizes:\n")
## Lexicon sizes:
purrr::iwalk(LEXICONS, ~ cat(sprintf("  %-20s %d terms\n", .y, length(.x))))
##   imaginary            20 terms
##   infrastructural      20 terms
##   data                 19 terms
##   rights_ethics        20 terms
##   development          20 terms
##   sovereignty          14 terms

LEXICON SCORING

For each document, I count the raw frequency of each lexicon term using regex word-boundary matching, then normalise by document word count.

The result is a density score: occurrences per word. This is the same normalisation strategy used in the Python pipeline (Cell 14).

I use purrr::map_dfr to iterate cleanly over the lexicon list without explicit loops. The inner sapply counts matches for all terms in one category, sums them, and divides by word count.

IMPORTANT: I convert all text to lowercase before matching because the cleaning pipeline already lowercased everything. The regex flag ignore.case = TRUE is a safety net, not the primary mechanism.

score_lexicon <- function(text, terms) {

  # Guard against empty or NA text
  if (is.na(text) || nchar(text) == 0) return(0)

  text_lower  <- tolower(text)
  word_count  <- length(str_split(text_lower, "\\s+")[[1]])

  if (word_count == 0) return(0)

  # Count each term independently using word boundaries
  raw_count <- sum(sapply(terms, function(term) {
    pattern <- paste0("\\b", str_replace_all(term, " ", "\\\\s+"), "\\b")
    length(regmatches(text_lower, gregexpr(pattern, text_lower, perl = TRUE))[[1]])
  }))

  raw_count / word_count
}

# I apply the scorer to every document and every lexicon category.
# purrr::map_dfc produces one column per lexicon, all bound together cleanly.

lexicon_scores <- purrr::map_dfc(
  names(LEXICONS),
  function(cat_name) {
    scores <- sapply(df$clean_text, function(txt) {
      score_lexicon(txt, LEXICONS[[cat_name]])
    })
    tibble(!!cat_name := scores)
  }
)

# I bind the scores back onto the metadata.
# This is my primary analysis dataframe for all downstream comparisons.

df_lex <- bind_cols(
  df %>% select(filename, region, doc_type),
  lexicon_scores
)

# Quick inspection
cat("Lexicon scoring complete.\n")
## Lexicon scoring complete.
print(head(df_lex))
## # A tibble: 6 × 9
##   filename       region doc_type imaginary infrastructural    data rights_ethics
##   <chr>          <fct>  <fct>        <dbl>           <dbl>   <dbl>         <dbl>
## 1 EU AI Act _Re… EU     act       0.00101          0.00334 0.00780       0.00904
## 2 Botswana_data… Africa act       0.000213         0.00192 0.113         0.0127 
## 3 Cape-Verde_Da… Africa act       0                0.00347 0.0790        0.0107 
## 4 Cote-dIvoire_… Africa act       0.000311         0.00513 0.0819        0.0131 
## 5 Eswatini_Data… Africa act       0.000197         0.00158 0.0770        0.00739
## 6 Ethiopia_ Per… Africa act       0.000176         0.00132 0.0640        0.00562
## # ℹ 2 more variables: development <dbl>, sovereignty <dbl>

REGIONAL AVERAGES TABLE

This is the first descriptive step in testing both hypotheses. I compute mean normalised lexicon density per region across all six categories.

For H1, the imaginary and data rows are the key comparison: if the Brussels Effect is operating through discursive templates, African and EU documents should show similar density profiles on these vocabulary sets. Divergence in the imaginary row would suggest African strategies have developed their own aspirational framing rather than reproducing EU language wholesale.

For H2, the infrastructural and sovereignty rows are the direct test: H2 predicts Africa scores higher on both, reflecting the asymmetric material conditions under which African governance is being written. The ratio column (Africa / EU) makes this comparison immediately readable.

regional_means <- df_lex %>%
  group_by(region) %>%
  summarise(across(all_of(names(LEXICONS)), mean, na.rm = TRUE), .groups = "drop") %>%
  pivot_longer(cols = -region, names_to = "lexicon", values_to = "mean_density")

# Format as a wide table for display
regional_table <- regional_means %>%
  pivot_wider(names_from = region, values_from = mean_density) %>%
  mutate(
    difference      = Africa - EU,
    # Safe ratio: when EU = 0 the ratio is undefined; replace Inf/NaN with NA
    # so the table renders cleanly rather than showing Inf.
    ratio_africa_eu = if_else(EU > 0, Africa / EU, NA_real_)
  ) %>%
  arrange(desc(abs(difference)))

kable(
  regional_table %>%
    mutate(across(where(is.numeric), ~ round(.x, 5))),
  caption = "Mean normalised lexicon density by region",
  col.names = c("Lexicon", "Africa", "EU", "Difference (Africa − EU)", "Ratio (Africa / EU)")
) %>%
  kable_styling(
    bootstrap_options = c("striped", "hover", "condensed", "responsive"),
    full_width = FALSE
  ) %>%
  row_spec(which(regional_table$difference > 0), background = "#EEF4FF") %>%
  row_spec(which(regional_table$difference < 0), background = "#F0F8FF")
Mean normalised lexicon density by region
Lexicon Africa EU Difference (Africa − EU) Ratio (Africa / EU)
data 0.06931 0.03903 0.03029 1.77603
development 0.01056 0.00388 0.00668 2.72296
imaginary 0.00584 0.00066 0.00518 8.82385
infrastructural 0.00593 0.00242 0.00351 2.44776
sovereignty 0.00393 0.00229 0.00164 1.71926
rights_ethics 0.01087 0.01000 0.00088 1.08759

VISUALISATION: LEXICON DENSITY BY REGION

I use a grouped bar chart because it allows direct visual comparison of Africa vs EU for each lexicon category side by side.

The dark blue gradient palette is applied: Africa takes the deeper tone, EU the lighter. Consistent with the design palette defined at the top.

I reorder lexicon categories by the magnitude of the Africa score so that the most Africa-dominant categories appear first, making the visual argument immediately legible.

plot_lex <- regional_means %>%
  mutate(
    lexicon = fct_reorder(
      lexicon,
      ifelse(region == "Africa", mean_density, 0),
      .fun = sum
    )
  )

ggplot(plot_lex, aes(x = lexicon, y = mean_density, fill = region)) +
  geom_col(position = position_dodge(width = 0.7), width = 0.6) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = region_colours, name = "Region") +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::label_number(accuracy = 0.0001)) +
  labs(
    title    = "Lexicon Density Across Imaginary Categories",
    subtitle = "Normalised frequency per word — African and EU AI governance documents",
    x        = "Imaginary category",
    y        = "Mean density (count / word count)"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13) +
  theme(
    plot.title       = element_text(colour = pal$dark1, face = "bold"),
    plot.subtitle    = element_text(colour = pal$mid2),
    axis.text.x      = element_text(angle = 30, hjust = 1, colour = pal$dark2),
    axis.title       = element_text(colour = pal$dark2),
    legend.position  = "top",
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    panel.background = element_rect(fill = pal$neutral, colour = NA),
    plot.background  = element_rect(fill = "white", colour = NA)
  )

Mean normalised lexicon density by region and category

TF-IDF COSINE SIMILARITY — TESTING H1

H1 predicts high lexical and phraseological convergence between African AI strategies and EU governance frameworks, evidenced by shared governance vocabulary consistent with a Brussels Effect in discursive templates.

TF-IDF cosine similarity is my primary measure for this because it weights terms by their distinctiveness across the corpus rather than raw frequency. A high cosine score between an African document and an EU document means they share not just common words but the specific vocabulary that differentiates those documents from the rest of the corpus — precisely the kind of discursive template adoption H1 predicts.

Both corpora are fitted to the SAME vocabulary space using a shared vocabulary object so that African and EU documents are compared on equal terms. I use text2vec rather than tidytext’s bind_tf_idf because text2vec produces a sparse matrix that can be passed directly to sim2() for pairwise cosine computation without the memory cost of pivoting to a dense matrix. Steps: tokenise all documents, build and prune shared vocabulary, construct TF-IDF document-term matrix, split by region, compute pairwise cosine similarity, derive per-document averages.

tokens_all <- itoken(
  df$clean_text,
  preprocessor  = tolower,
  tokenizer     = word_tokenizer,
  progressbar   = FALSE
)

# Step 2: Build vocabulary — fitted on full corpus for shared space
# I prune very rare terms (min 2 docs) and overly common terms (> 85% of docs)
# These thresholds mirror the Python TF-IDF settings in Cell 7.

vocab <- create_vocabulary(tokens_all) %>%
  prune_vocabulary(
    term_count_min  = 2,
    doc_proportion_max = 0.85
  )

vectoriser <- vocab_vectorizer(vocab)

# Step 3: Document-term matrix with TF-IDF weighting
dtm_all <- create_dtm(tokens_all, vectoriser)

tfidf_transformer <- TfIdf$new()
dtm_tfidf <- tfidf_transformer$fit_transform(dtm_all)

# Step 4: Separate Africa and EU index positions
africa_idx <- which(df$region == "Africa")
eu_idx     <- which(df$region == "EU")

africa_tfidf <- dtm_tfidf[africa_idx, ]
eu_tfidf     <- dtm_tfidf[eu_idx,     ]

# Step 5: Cosine similarity matrix (Africa × EU)
# Each row = one African document; each column = one EU document.
# The value = lexical overlap in TF-IDF space.

sim_matrix <- sim2(africa_tfidf, eu_tfidf, method = "cosine", norm = "l2")

# Assign row/col names for readability
rownames(sim_matrix) <- df$filename[africa_idx]
colnames(sim_matrix) <- df$filename[eu_idx]

# Step 6: Average similarity per African document
africa_avg_sim <- rowMeans(sim_matrix)

# I add this back to the analysis dataframe
df_africa <- df[africa_idx, ] %>%
  mutate(avg_tfidf_similarity = africa_avg_sim)

cat("Cosine similarity matrix computed.\n")
## Cosine similarity matrix computed.
cat("Dimensions:", nrow(sim_matrix), "African docs ×", ncol(sim_matrix), "EU docs\n")
## Dimensions: 29 African docs × 2 EU docs
cat("\nAfrican document average TF-IDF similarity to EU:\n")
## 
## African document average TF-IDF similarity to EU:
print(summary(africa_avg_sim))
##    Min. 1st Qu.  Median    Mean 3rd Qu.    Max. 
## 0.04007 0.13134 0.19041 0.20098 0.29594 0.34256

HEATMAP: LEXICAL SIMILARITY MATRIX

The heatmap displays the full pairwise cosine similarity matrix between every African document and every EU document. Clustering groups African documents that are lexically similar to each other, revealing whether convergence on EU vocabulary is uniform across the African corpus or concentrated in a subset of strategies. Under a strong Brussels Effect (H1) I would expect a cluster of African documents with consistently higher similarity scores to EU instruments — particularly frameworks and acts that draw directly on the EU regulatory model. Colour runs from near-black navy at low similarity to pale sky at high similarity.

# pheatmap requires variance across the matrix values — if all cells are
# identical (which happens with the synthetic placeholder corpus), the colour
# scale breaks are not unique and the function throws an error.
# I check for this before calling pheatmap and fall back to a ggplot tile
# heatmap, which handles flat matrices gracefully.

# sim_matrix is a sparse dgCMatrix from text2vec.
# pheatmap and ggplot both require a dense base R matrix, so I convert
# immediately before any operation on the matrix values.
sim_dense <- as.matrix(sim_matrix)

has_variance <- diff(range(sim_dense, na.rm = TRUE)) > 1e-10
enough_docs  <- nrow(sim_dense) > 0 && ncol(sim_dense) > 0

if (enough_docs && has_variance) {

  do_cluster_rows <- nrow(sim_dense) > 2
  do_cluster_cols <- ncol(sim_dense) > 2

  pheatmap(
    sim_dense,
    color            = heatmap_palette,
    border_color     = NA,
    cluster_rows     = do_cluster_rows,
    cluster_cols     = do_cluster_cols,
    main             = "Lexical Similarity (TF-IDF Cosine) — Africa vs EU Documents",
    fontsize_row     = 7,
    fontsize_col     = 9,
    angle_col        = 45,
    display_numbers  = TRUE,
    number_format    = "%.3f",
    number_color     = "white",
    legend           = TRUE,
    silent           = FALSE
  )

} else if (enough_docs && !has_variance) {

  # Flat matrix fallback when all values are identical.
  sim_long <- as.data.frame(sim_dense) %>%
    rownames_to_column("Africa_doc") %>%
    pivot_longer(-Africa_doc, names_to = "EU_doc", values_to = "similarity")

  ggplot(sim_long, aes(x = EU_doc, y = Africa_doc, fill = similarity)) +
    geom_tile(colour = "white") +
    geom_text(aes(label = round(similarity, 3)), size = 3, colour = "white") +
    scale_fill_gradientn(colours = heatmap_palette, name = "Cosine\nsimilarity") +
    labs(
      title    = "Lexical Similarity (TF-IDF Cosine) — Africa vs EU Documents",
      subtitle = "Note: low variance across values",
      x = "EU documents", y = "African documents"
    ) +
    theme_minimal(base_size = 11) +
    theme(
      axis.text.x      = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1, size = 8),
      axis.text.y      = element_text(size = 8),
      plot.title       = element_text(colour = pal$dark1, face = "bold"),
      plot.subtitle    = element_text(colour = pal$mid2, size = 9),
      panel.background = element_rect(fill = pal$neutral, colour = NA)
    )

} else {
  message("Not enough documents to render similarity heatmap.")
}

TF-IDF cosine similarity between African and EU documents

TF-IDF DISTINCTIVE TERMS PER REGION

Rather than asserting which vocabulary characterises each region, I derive it empirically. I fit TF-IDF treating each region as a single pooled document, then extract the highest-scoring terms. These are the words that are frequent in one region but rare or absent in the other — the terms that most distinctively mark each corpus.

For H1, if African strategies are reproducing EU governance templates, the Africa-distinctive terms should overlap substantially with the EU-distinctive terms. Divergence — particularly if the Africa column is dominated by infrastructural and developmental language while the EU column is dominated by rights, risk, and regulatory terms — would indicate that even where surface-level vocabulary converges, the underlying imaginary is different. This is the empirical grounding for the lexicon categories above.

word_tokens <- df %>%
  select(filename, region, doc_type, clean_text) %>%
  unnest_tokens(word, clean_text) %>%
  anti_join(stop_words, by = "word") %>%
  filter(
    str_detect(word, "^[a-z]{3,}$"),   # only lowercase alpha, min 3 chars
    !str_detect(word, "^[ivxlcdm]+$")  # remove Roman numeral artefacts
  )

# Compute TF-IDF treating each region as a "document"
region_tf_idf <- word_tokens %>%
  count(region, word, name = "n") %>%
  bind_tf_idf(word, region, n) %>%
  arrange(region, desc(tf_idf))

# Extract top 20 terms per region
top_terms <- region_tf_idf %>%
  group_by(region) %>%
  slice_max(tf_idf, n = 20) %>%
  ungroup()

# Display as table
kable(
  top_terms %>%
    select(region, word, n, tf, idf, tf_idf) %>%
    mutate(across(where(is.numeric), ~ round(.x, 4))),
  caption = "Top 20 TF-IDF distinctive terms per region"
) %>%
  kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped", "hover", "condensed"))
Top 20 TF-IDF distinctive terms per region
region word n tf idf tf_idf
Africa subsection 777 0.0035 0.6931 0.0024
Africa gazette 452 0.0020 0.6931 0.0014
Africa regulator 405 0.0018 0.6931 0.0013
Africa africa 404 0.0018 0.6931 0.0013
Africa commissioner 380 0.0017 0.6931 0.0012
Africa ghana 374 0.0017 0.6931 0.0012
Africa amakuru 345 0.0016 0.6931 0.0011
Africa bwite 334 0.0015 0.6931 0.0010
Africa strategic 305 0.0014 0.6931 0.0010
Africa des 301 0.0014 0.6931 0.0009
Africa kenya 272 0.0012 0.6931 0.0009
Africa egypt 256 0.0012 0.6931 0.0008
Africa cyangwa 246 0.0011 0.6931 0.0008
Africa nigeria 246 0.0011 0.6931 0.0008
Africa traitement 243 0.0011 0.6931 0.0008
Africa south 233 0.0011 0.6931 0.0007
Africa minister 222 0.0010 0.6931 0.0007
Africa african 218 0.0010 0.6931 0.0007
Africa talent 212 0.0010 0.6931 0.0007
Africa mauritius 211 0.0010 0.6931 0.0007

EU recall 8 0.0001 0.6931 0.0001

VISUALISATION: DISTINCTIVE TERMS BAR CHART

I use a faceted horizontal bar chart so both regions appear side by side. Terms within each region are ordered by TF-IDF score (highest at top). This mirrors the type of visualisation used in the Brussels Effect paper analyses and makes the imaginary/infrastructural split immediately visible when scanning the Africa column.

top_terms %>%
  mutate(word = reorder_within(word, tf_idf, region)) %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = word, y = tf_idf, fill = region)) +
  geom_col(show.legend = FALSE) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = region_colours) +
  scale_x_reordered() +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = label_number(accuracy = 0.001)) +
  facet_wrap(~ region, scales = "free_y") +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(
    title    = "Distinctively Weighted Terms by Region (TF-IDF)",
    subtitle = "Higher score = more characteristic of that region relative to the other",
    x        = NULL,
    y        = "TF-IDF weight"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
  theme(
    plot.title    = element_text(colour = pal$dark1, face = "bold"),
    plot.subtitle = element_text(colour = pal$mid2),
    strip.text    = element_text(colour = pal$dark2, face = "bold", size = 12),
    axis.text     = element_text(colour = pal$dark2),
    panel.background = element_rect(fill = pal$neutral, colour = NA)
  )

Top TF-IDF distinctive terms per region

IMAGINARY GAP — OPERATIONALISING THE ASYMMETRY IN H2

The imaginary gap operationalises the core asymmetry H2 predicts. It is defined as: imaginary density minus infrastructural density. A positive gap means a document invests more language in aspirational future-oriented framing than in the material conditions of deployment. A negative or small gap means infrastructural concerns dominate.

H2 predicts that African strategies will have a smaller or negative gap relative to EU instruments because they are written under conditions of material constraint — compute deficits, connectivity gaps, cloud dependency, contested data sovereignty — that EU instruments can afford to assume away. EU acts, by contrast, are written from a position of existing infrastructure and address regulatory architecture rather than material preconditions. I compute the gap at document level and aggregate by region to test this prediction directly.

df_gap <- df_lex %>%
  mutate(
    imaginary_gap = imaginary - infrastructural,
    imaginary_density     = imaginary,
    infrastructural_density = infrastructural
  )

gap_summary <- df_gap %>%
  group_by(region) %>%
  summarise(
    mean_imaginary_density      = mean(imaginary_density,       na.rm = TRUE),
    mean_infrastructural_density = mean(infrastructural_density, na.rm = TRUE),
    mean_imaginary_gap          = mean(imaginary_gap,           na.rm = TRUE),
    sd_imaginary_gap            = sd(imaginary_gap,             na.rm = TRUE),
    n_documents                 = n(),
    .groups = "drop"
  )

kable(
  gap_summary %>% mutate(across(where(is.numeric), ~ round(.x, 5))),
  caption = "Imaginary vs infrastructural density and gap by region"
) %>%
  kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped", "hover"))
Imaginary vs infrastructural density and gap by region
region mean_imaginary_density mean_infrastructural_density mean_imaginary_gap sd_imaginary_gap n_documents
Africa 0.00584 0.00593 -0.00009 0.00521 29
EU 0.00066 0.00242 -0.00176 0.00080 2

VISUALISATION: IMAGINARY GAP BY REGION

I use a grouped bar chart for the two density columns and overlay the gap as a point with error bars. This gives a richer picture than the Python notebook’s single bar chart and communicates the gap more directly.

gap_long <- gap_summary %>%
  pivot_longer(
    cols      = c(mean_imaginary_density, mean_infrastructural_density),
    names_to  = "measure",
    values_to = "value"
  ) %>%
  mutate(
    measure = recode(measure,
      mean_imaginary_density       = "Imaginary",
      mean_infrastructural_density = "Infrastructural"
    )
  )

ggplot(gap_long, aes(x = region, y = value, fill = measure)) +
  geom_col(position = position_dodge(0.7), width = 0.6) +
  geom_point(
    data    = gap_summary,
    mapping = aes(x = region, y = mean_imaginary_gap, fill = NULL),
    colour  = pal$dark1, size = 4, shape = 18
  ) +
  scale_fill_manual(
    values = c("Imaginary" = pal$mid1, "Infrastructural" = pal$light1),
    name   = "Lexicon"
  ) +
  labs(
    title    = "Imaginary versus Infrastructural Density by Region",
    subtitle = "Diamond marker = imaginary gap (imaginary − infrastructural)",
    x        = "Region",
    y        = "Mean normalised density"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13) +
  theme(
    plot.title    = element_text(colour = pal$dark1, face = "bold"),
    plot.subtitle = element_text(colour = pal$mid2),
    legend.position  = "top",
    panel.background = element_rect(fill = pal$neutral, colour = NA)
  )

Imaginary vs infrastructural density and gap by region

STATISTICAL TESTS — HYPOTHESIS 1

H1 predicts high lexical convergence between African AI strategies and EU governance frameworks, evidenced by shared governance vocabulary consistent with a Brussels Effect in discursive templates. I operationalise this through two tests:

First, I test whether imaginary lexicon density — the aspirational governance vocabulary that the Brussels Effect is predicted to export — differs significantly between regions. Under H1 I expect the difference to be small, indicating convergence. A large significant difference would challenge H1 because it would mean the two regions are investing in different vocabularies of technological futurity.

Second, I test all six lexicon categories simultaneously using Wilcoxon rank-sum tests (Mann-Whitney U). This non-parametric test is appropriate because policy document corpora are small and the distributions are right-skewed. I report effect size r = Z / sqrt(N) alongside p-values following Grimmer and Stewart (2013) and Laver et al. (2003).

run_wilcox <- function(data, variable, group_var = "region",
                       group1 = "Africa", group2 = "EU") {

  x1 <- data %>% filter(.data[[group_var]] == group1) %>% pull(.data[[variable]])
  x2 <- data %>% filter(.data[[group_var]] == group2) %>% pull(.data[[variable]])

  if (length(x1) < 2 || length(x2) < 2) {
    return(tibble(
      variable  = variable,
      statistic = NA, p_value = NA, effect_r = NA,
      direction = NA, n_africa = length(x1), n_eu = length(x2)
    ))
  }

  wt     <- wilcox.test(x1, x2, exact = FALSE, correct = TRUE)
  n_total <- length(x1) + length(x2)
  effect_r <- abs(qnorm(wt$p.value / 2)) / sqrt(n_total)

  tibble(
    variable  = variable,
    statistic = wt$statistic,
    p_value   = wt$p.value,
    effect_r  = effect_r,
    direction = ifelse(mean(x1, na.rm = TRUE) > mean(x2, na.rm = TRUE),
                       "Africa > EU", "EU > Africa"),
    n_africa  = length(x1),
    n_eu      = length(x2)
  )
}

# Run tests for all lexicon categories
wilcox_results <- purrr::map_dfr(names(LEXICONS), ~ run_wilcox(df_lex, .x))

# Add significance stars for legibility
wilcox_results <- wilcox_results %>%
  mutate(
    sig = case_when(
      p_value < 0.001 ~ "***",
      p_value < 0.01  ~ "**",
      p_value < 0.05  ~ "*",
      p_value < 0.1   ~ ".",
      TRUE            ~ "ns"
    )
  )

kable(
  wilcox_results %>%
    mutate(across(c(statistic, p_value, effect_r), ~ round(.x, 4))),
  caption = "Wilcoxon rank-sum tests: lexicon density differences between Africa and EU"
) %>%
  kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped", "hover")) %>%
  column_spec(4, bold = TRUE)
Wilcoxon rank-sum tests: lexicon density differences between Africa and EU
variable statistic p_value effect_r direction n_africa n_eu sig
imaginary 26 0.8407 0.0361 Africa > EU 29 2 ns
infrastructural 39 0.4449 0.1372 Africa > EU 29 2 ns
data 43 0.2777 0.1950 Africa > EU 29 2 ns
rights_ethics 29 1.0000 0.0000 Africa > EU 29 2 ns
development 28 0.9679 0.0072 Africa > EU 29 2 ns
sovereignty 23 0.6583 0.0794 Africa > EU 29 2 ns

STATISTICAL TESTS — HYPOTHESIS 2

H2 predicts that infrastructural imaginaries — references to compute, connectivity, cloud dependency, and sovereignty over technical substrate — will be more prominent in African strategies than in EU instruments, reflecting the asymmetric material conditions under which African governance is articulated. This is a directional hypothesis.

I test it with a one-sided Wilcoxon rank-sum test (alternative = “greater”) so the test is precisely aligned with the theoretical prediction rather than testing a generic difference. The effect size r = Z / sqrt(N) is reported to allow comparison with the H1 tests above and to give a magnitude estimate independent of sample size.

africa_infra <- df_lex %>% filter(region == "Africa") %>% pull(infrastructural)
eu_infra     <- df_lex %>% filter(region == "EU")     %>% pull(infrastructural)

wt_h2 <- wilcox.test(africa_infra, eu_infra,
                     alternative = "greater",
                     exact = FALSE, correct = TRUE)

# Effect size r = Z / sqrt(N).
# qnorm() returns Inf when p = 0 or p = 1 (exact boundary cases with small n).
# I clamp the z-score to a finite value in those cases so the output is clean.
n_h2      <- length(africa_infra) + length(eu_infra)
z_raw     <- qnorm(wt_h2$p.value)
z_h2      <- ifelse(is.finite(z_raw), z_raw, sign(z_raw) * qnorm(0.9999))
effect_h2 <- abs(z_h2) / sqrt(n_h2)

cat("Hypothesis 2 — One-sided Wilcoxon test (Africa > EU on infrastructural):\n")
## Hypothesis 2 — One-sided Wilcoxon test (Africa > EU on infrastructural):
cat(sprintf("  W = %.1f\n", wt_h2$statistic))
##   W = 39.0
cat(sprintf("  p-value (one-sided) = %.4f\n", wt_h2$p.value))
##   p-value (one-sided) = 0.2225
cat(sprintf("  Effect size r = %.4f\n", effect_h2))
##   Effect size r = 0.1372
cat(sprintf("  Mean Africa: %.5f | Mean EU: %.5f\n",
            mean(africa_infra, na.rm = TRUE),
            mean(eu_infra,     na.rm = TRUE)))
##   Mean Africa: 0.00593 | Mean EU: 0.00242

H1 TARGETED TESTS: AFRICAN AI FRAMEWORKS vs EU AI ACT / AFRICAN DATA ACTS vs GDPR

The previous regression model produced a structurally unestimable interaction term (regionEU:doc_typeframework = NA) because there are no EU framework documents in this corpus — the EU instruments are acts only, which is correct by design. A generic region × doc_type model is also the wrong tool for H1, which makes two specific directional predictions about two specific document-type pairings.

H1a tests African AI frameworks against the EU AI Act on imaginary vocabulary — the aspirational governance language the Brussels Effect is predicted to export. H1a predicts Africa scores lower because African strategies articulate technological futures through a developmental and infrastructural register rather than reproducing the EU’s frontier imaginary.

H1b tests African data acts against the GDPR on data/DP vocabulary — the regulatory language where the Brussels Effect is most documented. H1b predicts Africa scores higher because GDPR-derived vocabulary (consent, data subject, controller, processing) has diffused into African AI regulatory frameworks through direct legislative borrowing.

# H1 requires two targeted directional comparisons, not a generic
# region × doc_type interaction model. The previous regression produced
# a structurally unestimable interaction (regionEU:doc_typeframework = NA)
# because there are no EU framework documents in this corpus by design.
#
# The correct operationalisation of H1 is:
#
#   Comparison 1 — IMAGINARY vocabulary
#   African AI frameworks  vs  EU AI Act
#   H1a predicts: Africa imaginary < EU imaginary
#   Rationale: African strategies do not reproduce the EU's aspirational
#   futures register because that register assumes existing infrastructure.
#
#   Comparison 2 — DATA / DP vocabulary
#   African data acts  vs  EU GDPR (EU acts)
#   H1b predicts: Africa data > EU data
#   Rationale: GDPR-derived DP vocabulary has diffused into African AI
#   strategies through the Brussels Effect — this is where discursive
#   template adoption is strongest.
#
# I use one-sided Wilcoxon tests aligned precisely with each prediction.

africa_frameworks <- df_lex %>% filter(region == "Africa", doc_type == "framework")
africa_acts       <- df_lex %>% filter(region == "Africa", doc_type == "act")
eu_acts           <- df_lex %>% filter(region == "EU",     doc_type == "act")

# Comparison 1: imaginary density — African AI frameworks vs EU AI Act
wt_imaginary <- wilcox.test(
  africa_frameworks$imaginary,
  eu_acts$imaginary,
  alternative = "less",      # H1a: Africa imaginary < EU
  exact = FALSE, correct = TRUE
)

# Comparison 2: data/DP density — African data acts vs GDPR
wt_data <- wilcox.test(
  africa_acts$data,
  eu_acts$data,
  alternative = "greater",   # H1b: Africa data > EU (Brussels Effect absorption)
  exact = FALSE, correct = TRUE
)

# Effect size r = Z / sqrt(N), clamped against Inf for boundary p-values
safe_effect <- function(p, n) {
  z <- qnorm(p)
  z <- ifelse(is.finite(z), z, sign(z) * qnorm(0.9999))
  abs(z) / sqrt(n)
}

n_c1 <- nrow(africa_frameworks) + nrow(eu_acts)
n_c2 <- nrow(africa_acts)       + nrow(eu_acts)

h1_results <- tibble(
  Comparison  = c(
    "H1a: African AI frameworks vs EU AI Act — imaginary density",
    "H1b: African data acts vs GDPR — data/DP density"
  ),
  Prediction  = c(
    "Africa < EU (imaginary)",
    "Africa > EU (data/DP)"
  ),
  W           = c(wt_imaginary$statistic, wt_data$statistic),
  p_one_sided = c(wt_imaginary$p.value,   wt_data$p.value),
  effect_r    = c(safe_effect(wt_imaginary$p.value, n_c1),
                  safe_effect(wt_data$p.value,      n_c2)),
  mean_Africa = c(
    round(mean(africa_frameworks$imaginary, na.rm = TRUE), 5),
    round(mean(africa_acts$data,            na.rm = TRUE), 5)
  ),
  mean_EU     = c(
    round(mean(eu_acts$imaginary, na.rm = TRUE), 5),
    round(mean(eu_acts$data,      na.rm = TRUE), 5)
  )
) %>%
  mutate(
    sig = case_when(
      p_one_sided < 0.001 ~ "***",
      p_one_sided < 0.01  ~ "**",
      p_one_sided < 0.05  ~ "*",
      p_one_sided < 0.1   ~ ".",
      TRUE                ~ "ns"
    ),
    supported = case_when(
      Comparison == "H1a: African AI frameworks vs EU AI Act — imaginary density" ~
        ifelse(mean_Africa < mean_EU, "Direction correct", "Direction reversed"),
      Comparison == "H1b: African data acts vs GDPR — data/DP density" ~
        ifelse(mean_Africa > mean_EU, "Direction correct", "Direction reversed")
    )
  )

kable(
  h1_results %>% mutate(across(c(W, p_one_sided, effect_r), ~ round(.x, 4))),
  caption = "H1 directional tests: African AI frameworks vs EU AI Act (imaginary) and African data acts vs GDPR (data/DP)"
) %>%
  kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped", "hover", "condensed")) %>%
  column_spec(8, bold = TRUE)
H1 directional tests: African AI frameworks vs EU AI Act (imaginary) and African data acts vs GDPR (data/DP)
Comparison Prediction W p_one_sided effect_r mean_Africa mean_EU sig supported
H1a: African AI frameworks vs EU AI Act — imaginary density Africa < EU (imaginary) 18 0.9874 0.6751 0.01810 0.00066 ns Direction reversed
H1b: African data acts vs GDPR — data/DP density Africa > EU (data/DP) 36 0.0383 0.3774 0.09509 0.03903
Direction correct

VISUALISATION: DISTRIBUTION OF KEY LEXICON DENSITIES

The violin plot shows the full distribution shape for the three lexicons most central to H1 and H2: imaginary (H1 convergence test), infrastructural (H2 primary test), and data (H1 Brussels Effect in data governance). Violin width encodes the probability density at each score level. The inner boxplot marks the median and IQR. Individual documents are overlaid as jittered points so that outliers and cluster structure are visible — important in a corpus of 31 documents where individual texts carry weight.

df_lex_long <- df_lex %>%
  select(filename, region, doc_type,
         imaginary, infrastructural, data) %>%
  pivot_longer(cols = c(imaginary, infrastructural, data),
               names_to = "lexicon", values_to = "density")

ggplot(df_lex_long, aes(x = region, y = density, fill = region, colour = region)) +
  geom_violin(alpha = 0.35, trim = TRUE, scale = "width") +
  geom_boxplot(width = 0.15, outlier.shape = NA, fill = "white", alpha = 0.7) +
  geom_jitter(width = 0.08, size = 1.2, alpha = 0.7) +
  scale_fill_manual(values   = region_colours, guide = "none") +
  scale_colour_manual(values = region_colours, guide = "none") +
  facet_wrap(~ lexicon, scales = "free_y",
             labeller = labeller(lexicon = c(
               imaginary       = "Imaginary",
               infrastructural = "Infrastructural",
               data            = "Data"
             ))) +
  labs(
    title    = "Distribution of Key Lexicon Densities by Region",
    subtitle = "Violin = full distribution; box = IQR; points = individual documents",
    x        = "Region",
    y        = "Normalised density"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13) +
  theme(
    plot.title       = element_text(colour = pal$dark1, face = "bold"),
    plot.subtitle    = element_text(colour = pal$mid2),
    strip.text       = element_text(colour = pal$dark2, face = "bold"),
    panel.background = element_rect(fill = pal$neutral, colour = NA)
  )

Distribution of lexicon densities by region

TF-IDF SIMILARITY DECOMPOSED BY DOCUMENT TYPE

H1 concerns visions of technological futures, which are primarily encoded in strategy frameworks rather than in legislative acts. Acts are more procedural and prescriptive; high similarity between African acts and EU acts may simply reflect shared legal drafting conventions rather than genuine discursive template adoption. I therefore decompose average TF-IDF similarity by document type to test whether convergence is stronger in frameworks — where imaginary language is concentrated — than in acts. Under a strong H1, framework similarity should exceed act similarity.

africa_meta <- df[africa_idx, ] %>%
  select(filename, region, doc_type) %>%
  mutate(avg_tfidf_similarity = africa_avg_sim)

sim_by_type <- africa_meta %>%
  group_by(doc_type) %>%
  summarise(
    mean_sim = mean(avg_tfidf_similarity, na.rm = TRUE),
    sd_sim   = sd(avg_tfidf_similarity,   na.rm = TRUE),
    n        = n(),
    .groups  = "drop"
  )

kable(
  sim_by_type %>% mutate(across(where(is.numeric), ~ round(.x, 4))),
  caption = "Mean TF-IDF similarity to EU documents by African document type"
) %>%
  kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped", "hover"))
Mean TF-IDF similarity to EU documents by African document type
doc_type mean_sim sd_sim n
act 0.1649 0.0798 20
framework 0.2813 0.0590 9 
# Bar chart
ggplot(africa_meta,
       aes(x = doc_type, y = avg_tfidf_similarity, fill = doc_type)) +
  geom_col(stat = "summary", fun = "mean", width = 0.5) +
  geom_errorbar(
    stat = "summary", fun.data = mean_se,
    width = 0.2, colour = pal$dark1
  ) +
  geom_jitter(width = 0.1, size = 2, colour = pal$dark2, alpha = 0.6) +
  scale_fill_manual(
    values = c("act" = pal$mid1, "framework" = pal$light1),
    guide  = "none"
  ) +
  labs(
    title    = "African Document Similarity to EU by Document Type",
    subtitle = "Mean ± SE across all EU documents; points = individual African documents",
    x        = "Document type",
    y        = "Mean TF-IDF cosine similarity to EU"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13) +
  theme(
    plot.title       = element_text(colour = pal$dark1, face = "bold"),
    plot.subtitle    = element_text(colour = pal$mid2),
    panel.background = element_rect(fill = pal$neutral, colour = NA)
  )

Data vs Imaginary Density — Quadrant Analysis

This scatter plot provides a joint view of the two central dimensions of H1. The x-axis is imaginary density (the aspirational governance vocabulary H1 predicts will converge) and the y-axis is data density (the regulatory data governance vocabulary H1 predicts the Brussels Effect exports most strongly through GDPR and the Data Act). Documents cluster into quadrants that reveal their imaginary profile. Upper-right documents are high on both — fully converged with the EU regulatory imaginary. Upper-left documents are data-heavy but not imaginary-heavy — technical legal instruments. Lower-right are vision-heavy but not data-governed — aspirational strategies. If H1 holds, African frameworks should cluster near EU documents in this space.

ggplot(df_lex, aes(x = imaginary, y = data, colour = region, label = filename)) +
  geom_point(size = 3, alpha = 0.8) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = TRUE, linetype = "dashed", size = 0.7) +
  scale_colour_manual(values = region_colours, name = "Region") +
  labs(
    title    = "Data Governance Density vs Imaginary Density by Region",
    subtitle = "Each point = one document; line = within-region linear trend",
    x        = "Imaginary lexicon density",
    y        = "Data lexicon density"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13) +
  theme(
    plot.title       = element_text(colour = pal$dark1, face = "bold"),
    plot.subtitle    = element_text(colour = pal$mid2),
    legend.position  = "top",
    panel.background = element_rect(fill = pal$neutral, colour = NA)
  )

Data density vs imaginary density by region

H1 Extension: N-gram Overlap and Phrase-Level Convergence

H1 specifically claims evidence of shared governance vocabulary and verbatim or near-verbatim n-gram reproduction consistent with Brussels Effect discursive templates. TF-IDF cosine similarity captures general lexical overlap but cannot confirm phrase-level reproduction — two documents could share individual words while using completely different governance phrases.

I test the phrase-level dimension of H1 by extracting bigrams and trigrams from each regional corpus, computing what proportion of EU’s most frequent governance phrases also appear in African documents, and identifying the phrases that are shared versus those exclusive to each corpus. High overlap in governance trigrams (e.g. “artificial intelligence systems”, “risk management framework”, “fundamental rights protection”) would constitute direct evidence of discursive template adoption. Region-exclusive phrases reveal where the two corpora diverge in their framing despite surface lexical similarity.

# I tokenise to bigrams and trigrams separately using tidytext.
# unnest_tokens with token = "ngrams" and n = 2 or 3 produces
# contiguous word sequences. I remove any ngram that contains a
# stop word in either position because function-word sequences
# ("of the", "in a") would dominate and obscure substantive overlap.

bigrams <- df %>%
  select(filename, region, clean_text) %>%
  unnest_tokens(ngram, clean_text, token = "ngrams", n = 2) %>%
  separate(ngram, into = c("w1", "w2"), sep = " ", remove = FALSE) %>%
  filter(!w1 %in% stop_words$word, !w2 %in% stop_words$word) %>%
  select(filename, region, ngram)

trigrams <- df %>%
  select(filename, region, clean_text) %>%
  unnest_tokens(ngram, clean_text, token = "ngrams", n = 3) %>%
  separate(ngram, into = c("w1", "w2", "w3"), sep = " ", remove = FALSE) %>%
  filter(!w1 %in% stop_words$word, !w3 %in% stop_words$word) %>%
  select(filename, region, ngram)

# Frequency tables per region
africa_bigrams <- bigrams %>%
  filter(region == "Africa") %>%
  count(ngram, sort = TRUE) %>%
  mutate(normalised = n / sum(n))

eu_bigrams <- bigrams %>%
  filter(region == "EU") %>%
  count(ngram, sort = TRUE) %>%
  mutate(normalised = n / sum(n))

africa_trigrams <- trigrams %>%
  filter(region == "Africa") %>%
  count(ngram, sort = TRUE) %>%
  mutate(normalised = n / sum(n))

eu_trigrams <- trigrams %>%
  filter(region == "EU") %>%
  count(ngram, sort = TRUE) %>%
  mutate(normalised = n / sum(n))

# Shared ngrams: appear in BOTH Africa and EU corpora
shared_bigrams <- inner_join(
  africa_bigrams %>% rename(n_africa = n, norm_africa = normalised),
  eu_bigrams     %>% rename(n_eu = n,     norm_eu     = normalised),
  by = "ngram"
) %>%
  mutate(mean_freq = (norm_africa + norm_eu) / 2) %>%
  arrange(desc(mean_freq))

shared_trigrams <- inner_join(
  africa_trigrams %>% rename(n_africa = n, norm_africa = normalised),
  eu_trigrams     %>% rename(n_eu = n,     norm_eu     = normalised),
  by = "ngram"
) %>%
  mutate(mean_freq = (norm_africa + norm_eu) / 2) %>%
  arrange(desc(mean_freq))

# Overlap ratio: what proportion of EU's top ngrams also appear in Africa?
eu_top200_bigrams  <- eu_bigrams  %>% slice_max(n, n = 200) %>% pull(ngram)
eu_top200_trigrams <- eu_trigrams %>% slice_max(n, n = 200) %>% pull(ngram)

bigram_overlap_pct  <- mean(eu_top200_bigrams  %in% africa_bigrams$ngram)  * 100
trigram_overlap_pct <- mean(eu_top200_trigrams %in% africa_trigrams$ngram) * 100

cat("Bigram overlap (EU top 200 appearing in Africa):", round(bigram_overlap_pct, 1), "%
")
## Bigram overlap (EU top 200 appearing in Africa): 58.3 %
cat("Trigram overlap (EU top 200 appearing in Africa):", round(trigram_overlap_pct, 1), "%
")
## Trigram overlap (EU top 200 appearing in Africa): 41.7 %
cat("Shared bigrams found:", nrow(shared_bigrams), "
")
## Shared bigrams found: 2287
cat("Shared trigrams found:", nrow(shared_trigrams), "
")
## Shared trigrams found: 2361
# I display the top shared bigrams and trigrams separately.
# These are the specific governance phrases that both corpora use,
# which is the direct evidence H1 requires at the phrase level.

kable(
  head(shared_bigrams, 20) %>%
    mutate(across(where(is.numeric), ~ round(.x, 5))),
  caption = "Top 20 shared bigrams: phrases appearing in both African and EU documents"
) %>%
  kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped", "hover", "condensed"))
Top 20 shared bigrams: phrases appearing in both African and EU documents
ngram n_africa norm_africa n_eu norm_eu mean_freq
personal data 2669 0.02048 675 0.02595 0.02322
ai systems 201 0.00154 613 0.02357 0.01256
data subject 1680 0.01289 256 0.00984 0.01137
ai system 30 0.00023 488 0.01876 0.00950
data protection 1199 0.00920 201 0.00773 0.00846
risk ai 10 0.00008 428 0.01646 0.00827
supervisory authority 166 0.00127 328 0.01261 0.00694
data controller 1595 0.01224 9 0.00035 0.00629
purpose ai 1 0.00001 227 0.00873 0.00437
regulation eu 1 0.00001 199 0.00765 0.00383
natural persons 17 0.00013 195 0.00750 0.00381
data subjects 308 0.00236 132 0.00508 0.00372
ai models 35 0.00027 139 0.00534 0.00281
fundamental rights 44 0.00034 135 0.00519 0.00276
artificial intelligence 491 0.00377 36 0.00138 0.00258
personal information 640 0.00491 1 0.00004 0.00247
european union 18 0.00014 122 0.00469 0.00241
competent authorities 3 0.00002 124 0.00477 0.00240
supervisory authorities 11 0.00008 119 0.00458 0.00233
conformity assessment 1 0.00001 113 0.00434 0.00218 
kable(
  head(shared_trigrams, 20) %>%
    mutate(across(where(is.numeric), ~ round(.x, 5))),
  caption = "Top 20 shared trigrams: three-word governance phrases shared across regions"
) %>%
  kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped", "hover", "condensed"))
Top 20 shared trigrams: three-word governance phrases shared across regions
ngram n_africa norm_africa n_eu norm_eu mean_freq
risk ai systems 3 0.00002 247 0.00871 0.00437
processing of personal 482 0.00338 136 0.00480 0.00409
rights and freedoms 138 0.00097 95 0.00335 0.00216
controller or processor 59 0.00041 106 0.00374 0.00208
pursuant to article 1 0.00001 116 0.00409 0.00205
accordance with article 9 0.00006 108 0.00381 0.00194
protection of personal 221 0.00155 58 0.00205 0.00180
taking into account 62 0.00043 78 0.00275 0.00159
personal data breach 142 0.00099 31 0.00109 0.00104
data protection officer 112 0.00078 31 0.00109 0.00094
level of protection 53 0.00037 39 0.00138 0.00087
codes of conduct 60 0.00042 35 0.00123 0.00083
categories of personal 57 0.00040 33 0.00116 0.00078
pursuant to paragraph 2 0.00001 39 0.00138 0.00069
data protection impact 61 0.00043 27 0.00095 0.00069
technical and organisational 55 0.00039 28 0.00099 0.00069
protection impact assessment 55 0.00039 27 0.00095 0.00067
european data protection 1 0.00001 37 0.00130 0.00066
personal data protection 154 0.00108 5 0.00018 0.00063
natural or legal 25 0.00018 30 0.00106 0.00062 
# I also plot the bigrams most DISTINCTIVE to each region — those that
# appear in one corpus but not the other. This shows where the two
# corpora diverge in their framing, complementing the overlap evidence.

africa_only_bigrams <- africa_bigrams %>%
  filter(!ngram %in% eu_bigrams$ngram) %>%
  slice_max(n, n = 15) %>%
  mutate(region = "Africa")

eu_only_bigrams <- eu_bigrams %>%
  filter(!ngram %in% africa_bigrams$ngram) %>%
  slice_max(n, n = 15) %>%
  mutate(region = "EU")

distinctive_bigrams <- bind_rows(africa_only_bigrams, eu_only_bigrams) %>%
  mutate(ngram = reorder_within(ngram, n, region))

ggplot(distinctive_bigrams, aes(x = ngram, y = n, fill = region)) +
  geom_col(show.legend = FALSE) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = region_colours) +
  scale_x_reordered() +
  coord_flip() +
  facet_wrap(~ region, scales = "free_y") +
  labs(
    title    = "Region-Distinctive Bigrams",
    subtitle = "Bigrams appearing exclusively in one corpus — not shared with the other",
    x        = NULL,
    y        = "Raw frequency"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
  theme(
    plot.title       = element_text(colour = pal$dark1, face = "bold"),
    plot.subtitle    = element_text(colour = pal$mid2),
    strip.text       = element_text(colour = pal$dark2, face = "bold"),
    panel.background = element_rect(fill = pal$neutral, colour = NA)
  )

Most distinctive bigrams per region — divergence from shared vocabulary

H2 Extension: KWIC Density for H2 Target Terms

H2 names specific constructs — compute, connectivity, cloud dependency, and sovereignty over technical substrate — as the markers of infrastructural imaginaries in African governance discourse. The lexicon scoring above tests these as aggregate categories. Here I use keyword-in-context (KWIC) analysis to test each construct individually, at the exact surface forms named in the hypothesis, and to examine the documentary contexts in which they appear.

KWIC density (hits per 1000 words) provides a normalised frequency measure that is directly comparable across documents of different lengths. Examining the concordance lines for sovereignty and cloud terms also provides the qualitative grounding that quantitative frequency counts alone cannot offer — showing not just that African strategies mention these terms more, but how they frame them, which is the substance of the infrastructural imaginary argument.

# I build a quanteda corpus from the same clean_text used throughout,
# preserving the region and doc_type metadata as document variables.
# kwic() requires a tokens object; I tokenise without further stemming
# because I want to match the exact surface forms named in H2.

corp_kwic <- quanteda::corpus(
  df,
  text_field = "clean_text",
  docid_field = "filename"
)

quanteda::docvars(corp_kwic, "region")   <- df$region
quanteda::docvars(corp_kwic, "doc_type") <- df$doc_type

toks_kwic <- quanteda::tokens(
  corp_kwic,
  remove_punct   = FALSE,
  remove_numbers = FALSE
)

# H2 target terms — exactly the constructs named in the hypothesis.
# I use glob patterns (*) to capture morphological variants:
# "connect*" captures connectivity, connected, connection etc.
h2_terms <- list(
  compute        = c("comput*", "computing", "computational", "compute"),
  connectivity   = c("connect*", "broadband", "internet access", "bandwidth"),
  cloud          = c("cloud*", "cloud computing", "cloud infrastructure", "cloud dependency"),
  sovereignty    = c("sovereign*", "digital sovereignty", "data sovereignty", "strategic autonomy"),
  infrastructure = c("infrastructur*", "data center*", "server*", "hardware")
)

# Run kwic for each term group and compile results
kwic_results <- purrr::imap_dfr(h2_terms, function(patterns, term_name) {
  purrr::map_dfr(patterns, function(pat) {
    hits <- quanteda::kwic(toks_kwic, pattern = pat, valuetype = "glob", window = 5)
    if (nrow(hits) == 0) return(tibble())
    as_tibble(hits) %>%
      mutate(
        h2_term  = term_name,
        pattern  = pat,
        region   = quanteda::docvars(corp_kwic, "region")[
          match(docname, quanteda::docnames(corp_kwic))
        ],
        doc_type = quanteda::docvars(corp_kwic, "doc_type")[
          match(docname, quanteda::docnames(corp_kwic))
        ]
      )
  })
})

cat("Total KWIC hits across H2 terms:", nrow(kwic_results), "
")
## Total KWIC hits across H2 terms: 1604
print(table(kwic_results$h2_term, kwic_results$region))
##                 
##                  Africa  EU
##   cloud             135   1
##   compute           595  25
##   connectivity      245  13
##   infrastructure    513  42
##   sovereignty        35   0
# I compute normalised KWIC density: hits per 1000 words per document.
# This allows fair comparison across documents of different lengths.

doc_wordcounts <- df %>%
  mutate(word_count = lengths(str_split(clean_text, "\\s+"))) %>%
  select(filename, word_count, region, doc_type)

kwic_density <- kwic_results %>%
  count(docname, h2_term, region, doc_type, name = "hits") %>%
  left_join(doc_wordcounts, by = c("docname" = "filename",
                                    "region"   = "region",
                                    "doc_type" = "doc_type")) %>%
  mutate(density_per_1000 = hits / word_count * 1000)

# Regional averages per H2 term
kwic_regional <- kwic_density %>%
  group_by(region, h2_term) %>%
  summarise(
    mean_density = mean(density_per_1000, na.rm = TRUE),
    total_hits   = sum(hits),
    n_docs       = n(),
    .groups = "drop"
  )

kable(
  kwic_regional %>%
    pivot_wider(names_from = region,
                values_from = c(mean_density, total_hits, n_docs)) %>%
    mutate(across(where(is.numeric), ~ round(.x, 3))),
  caption = "KWIC density per 1000 words by H2 term and region"
) %>%
  kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped", "hover", "condensed"))
KWIC density per 1000 words by H2 term and region
h2_term mean_density_Africa mean_density_EU total_hits_Africa total_hits_EU n_docs_Africa n_docs_EU
cloud 1.015 0.011 135 1 9 1
compute 1.649 0.151 595 25 21 2
connectivity 0.485 0.102 245 13 28 2
infrastructure 1.301 0.247 513 42 20 2
sovereignty 0.227 NA 35 NA 7 NA 
ggplot(kwic_regional, aes(x = reorder(h2_term, mean_density), y = mean_density, fill = region)) +
  geom_col(position = position_dodge(0.7), width = 0.6) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = region_colours, name = "Region") +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(
    title    = "KWIC Density of H2 Infrastructural Terms",
    subtitle = "Mean occurrences per 1000 words — directly testing H2 term-by-term",
    x        = "H2 term category",
    y        = "Mean density per 1000 words"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13) +
  theme(
    plot.title       = element_text(colour = pal$dark1, face = "bold"),
    plot.subtitle    = element_text(colour = pal$mid2),
    legend.position  = "top",
    panel.background = element_rect(fill = pal$neutral, colour = NA)
  )

KWIC density for H2 infrastructural terms by region 

# I display a sample of raw KWIC concordance lines for the sovereignty
# and cloud terms — the two most theoretically loaded categories.
# This is the qualitative anchor that H1 and H2 both require:
# showing the actual words in their documentary context rather than
# only reporting aggregate statistics.

kwic_sample <- kwic_results %>%
  filter(h2_term %in% c("sovereignty", "cloud")) %>%
  select(docname, region, h2_term, pre, keyword, post) %>%
  group_by(h2_term, region) %>%
  slice_head(n = 5) %>%
  ungroup()

kable(
  kwic_sample,
  caption = "Sample KWIC concordance lines for sovereignty and cloud terms"
) %>%
  kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped", "hover", "condensed"), font_size = 11)
Sample KWIC concordance lines for sovereignty and cloud terms
docname region h2_term pre keyword post
Egypt_AIstrategy English 16-1-2025-1.pdf Africa cloud robust scalable ai infrastructure and cloud services innovate business models and
Egypt_AIstrategy English 16-1-2025-1.pdf Africa cloud the digital economy and ai cloud are the key enablers of
Egypt_AIstrategy English 16-1-2025-1.pdf Africa cloud speed connectivity data centers and cloud services to enable ai development
Egypt_AIstrategy English 16-1-2025-1.pdf Africa cloud robust scalable ai infrastructure and cloud services innovate business models and
Egypt_AIstrategy English 16-1-2025-1.pdf Africa cloud edge domestic data center intelligent cloud computing resource ubiquitous g and
EU AI Act _Regulation EU 20241689.pdf EU cloud fire alarm controlling systems in cloud computing centres the deployment of
Ethiopia_ Personal Data Protection Proclamation No 13212024.pdf Africa sovereignty አለበት ፯ የዳታ ማቀናበር ሕጋዊነት sovereignty of the data lawfulness of
Ethiopia_ Personal Data Protection Proclamation No 13212024.pdf Africa sovereignty ፳ ፪ የዳታ ሉዓላዊነት data sovereignty ፩ እያንዳንዱ የዳታ ተቆጣጣሪ ወይም
Nigeria_Nigeria Data Protection Act 2023 (NDPA).pdf Africa sovereignty of data subject or data sovereignty without approval of the national
Ghana-AI-Strategy.pdf Africa sovereignty ai while guaranteeing african ai sovereignty this calls for regional coordination
Ghana-AI-Strategy.pdf Africa sovereignty ai while guaranteeing african ai sovereignty this calls for regional coordination

Summary of Empirical Findings

I compile the key results for both hypotheses in a single table that can be read alongside the paper. For H1, I report the TF-IDF cosine similarity scores (primary measure) and the n-gram overlap percentage (phrase-level measure). For H2, I report the infrastructural lexicon density comparison and the Wilcoxon test result. Each row corresponds to a specific testable prediction derived from the hypothesis as stated.

results_summary <- tibble(
  Hypothesis = c(
    "H1a: Imaginary similarity Africa < EU (lower convergence on futures)",
    "H1b: Data similarity Africa < EU (lower convergence on data governance)",
    "H2: Infrastructural density Africa > EU"
  ),
  Test = c(
    "TF-IDF cosine similarity + Wilcoxon (imaginary density diff)",
    "Wilcoxon (data density diff)",
    "One-sided Wilcoxon (Africa > EU on infrastructural)"
  ),
  Result = c(
    paste0("Africa mean imaginary sim = ",
           round(mean(df_lex$imaginary[df_lex$region == "Africa"], na.rm = TRUE), 5),
           "; EU = ",
           round(mean(df_lex$imaginary[df_lex$region == "EU"],     na.rm = TRUE), 5)),
    paste0("Africa mean data = ",
           round(mean(df_lex$data[df_lex$region == "Africa"], na.rm = TRUE), 5),
           "; EU = ",
           round(mean(df_lex$data[df_lex$region == "EU"],     na.rm = TRUE), 5)),
    paste0("Africa mean infra = ",
           round(mean(df_lex$infrastructural[df_lex$region == "Africa"], na.rm = TRUE), 5),
           "; EU = ",
           round(mean(df_lex$infrastructural[df_lex$region == "EU"],     na.rm = TRUE), 5))
  )
)

kable(results_summary, caption = "Summary of empirical findings by hypothesis") %>%
  kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped", "hover", "condensed"))
Summary of empirical findings by hypothesis
Hypothesis Test Result
H1a: Imaginary similarity Africa < EU (lower convergence on futures) TF-IDF cosine similarity + Wilcoxon (imaginary density diff) Africa mean imaginary sim = 0.00584; EU = 0.00066
H1b: Data similarity Africa < EU (lower convergence on data governance) Wilcoxon (data density diff) Africa mean data = 0.06931; EU = 0.03903
H2: Infrastructural density Africa > EU One-sided Wilcoxon (Africa > EU on infrastructural) Africa mean infra = 0.00593; EU = 0.00242

Reproducibility: Session Information

I record the exact R and package versions used so that every analytical decision in this document — from the TF-IDF pruning thresholds to the Wilcoxon continuity correction — can be reproduced exactly. This is standard practice in computational text analysis (Grimmer & Stewart 2013) and allows the marker to verify that no packages or versions were substituted between drafts.

sessionInfo()

## [73] bit64_4.6.0-1       sylly.en_0.1-3
